medications.indication_rationale: Oral NSAIDs are first-line to reduce the pain and inflammation of superficial thrombophlebitis and may reduce local extension. Prescribed by the source clinician as a short course.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 400 mg orally three times a day with food
contraindications_in_this_case: Standard NSAID cautions apply — avoid with active peptic ulcer disease, significant renal impairment, or concurrent anticoagulation; take with food. None of these were flagged in the source, so confirm at the in-person review.

medications.indication_rationale: Reasonable adjunct or alternative for pain, but the patient reports it has given minimal relief for the inflammatory pain — an NSAID is the more appropriate anti-inflammatory first-line here.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1 g orally up to four times a day as needed

medications.indication_rationale: This is inflammatory superficial thrombophlebitis, not cellulitis — the patient is afebrile and the erythema is linear along a vein. Prescribing antibiotics is an error of commission that treats a mimic that is not present.

Anticoagulation may be indicated for extensive superficial thrombophlebitis or thrombus near the saphenofemoral junction, but that decision depends on venous duplex mapping of clot extent and must NOT be initiated remotely by a telehealth service. Starting it before imaging, or without in-person assessment, is unsafe; it belongs to the same-day clinician after duplex.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Elevate the legs above the level of the heart for about 20 minutes each evening to drain static venous blood and relieve pressure.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces venous hypertension and the aching/heaviness, and supports resolution of the inflammation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop wearing tight socks with constricting elastic bands, which impede venous return.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Constricting bands worsen venous stasis, the driver of both the varicosities and the thrombophlebitis.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a warm compress to the tender area for symptom relief.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Warmth eases the local discomfort of superficial thrombophlebitis.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Reduce uninterrupted prolonged standing where possible; take breaks to walk and elevate the legs.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prolonged standing at work drives the venous hypertension worsening the disease.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Once the acute inflammation settles, use properly fitted Class 2 graduated compression stockings during the day.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Graduated compression is the mainstay of managing chronic venous insufficiency and preventing recurrence; fit after the acute phase.

patient_education_points.point: One of your bulging surface veins has developed a small clot and inflammation (superficial thrombophlebitis). This is usually painful but not the dangerous deep clot — however, we cannot be certain over a video call, so an in-person check and an ultrasound are needed to be sure.
Anti-inflammatory medication and elevating your leg will settle the pain and swelling; take the medication with food.

Go to an emergency department or call an ambulance immediately if your whole calf suddenly swells, the redness spreads rapidly, or you develop chest pain or shortness of breath — these can signal a deep clot or a clot travelling to the lungs.
The underlying varicose veins may later need assessment for minor procedures; graduated compression stockings help once the acute soreness settles.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent same-day in-person assessment; then routine GP review within 1 week
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: In-person leg examination to confirm superficial thrombophlebitis and exclude DVT; arrange lower-limb venous duplex ultrasound; reassess response to NSAIDs and resolution of the cord; plan varicose vein management.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any spread of redness, increasing calf swelling, fever, or new chest pain/breathlessness warrants immediate emergency assessment rather than waiting for the routine review.

must_include_items: Recognise a new painful, firm, red cord over a varicosity as an acute complication (superficial thrombophlebitis), not just chronic varicose veins
State explicitly that a concurrent deep vein thrombosis cannot be excluded via telehealth and arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment plus venous duplex ultrasound
Provide interim symptomatic management — NSAID (ibuprofen) with food, leg elevation, warm compress, stop constricting socks
Give clear escalation triggers (spreading redness, massive calf swelling, chest pain/breathlessness)
acceptable_alternatives: Directing the patient to an urgent care clinic or ED same-day (rather than GP) for the in-person exam and duplex is acceptable, especially after-hours
Recommending paracetamol in addition to (not instead of) an NSAID for pain
errors_of_omission: Falsely reassuring the patient that this is benign varicose veins and deferring to routine review
Failing to arrange in-person assessment / venous duplex to exclude DVT
Omitting escalation/safety-net advice for DVT and PE
errors_of_commission: Prescribing antibiotics for presumed cellulitis in an afebrile patient with linear erythema
Self-initiating therapeutic anticoagulation remotely before duplex defines clot extent
Recommending fitting of compression stockings onto an acutely inflamed, tender leg before the inflammation settles
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognising the acute complication AND arranging urgent same-day in-person assessment with duplex to exclude DVT. Reassuring the patient it is benign and deferring to routine care is a critical under-triage failure regardless of other content.
